questions.title: What is the Virtual DOM and how does React use it?
questions.shortAnswer: The Virtual DOM is an in-memory tree of element descriptions. React diffs it against the previous tree (reconciliation) and applies the minimal set of changes to the real DOM.
questions.detailedExplanation: Rendering produces a tree of plain objects. The reconciler walks old vs new, deciding what to mount, update, or unmount. Keys are critical for list diffing.
questions.realWorldExample: Re-rendering a 10k-item list with stable keys updates only the changed rows; without keys, React re-renders many more.
commonMistakes: Index as key in dynamic lists
questions.interviewTip: Mention 'reconciliation' explicitly — it's the technical term interviewers listen for.

questions.title: useEffect — when does it run and how do dependencies work?
questions.shortAnswer: useEffect runs after commit. With [] it runs once; with [deps] it re-runs when any dep changes by Object.is comparison.
questions.detailedExplanation: Effects describe synchronization with the outside world. The cleanup runs before the next effect and on unmount. Missing deps cause stale closures.
questions.realWorldExample: Subscribing to a WebSocket: open in effect, close in cleanup. Re-subscribe when the URL dep changes.
bestPractices: Include all referenced values in deps
Move pure derivations out of effects
commonMistakes: Putting derived data in effects instead of useMemo
questions.interviewTip: Cite 'You Might Not Need an Effect' — shows you read the docs.

questions.title: useMemo vs useCallback.
questions.shortAnswer: useMemo caches a computed value; useCallback caches a function reference. Both rerun when deps change.
questions.detailedExplanation: useCallback(fn, d) is just useMemo(() => fn, d). Use them to keep referential equality for memoized children and effect deps — not as a default optimization.
questions.realWorldExample: Wrapping an onClick passed to a memoized list row prevents the whole list from re-rendering when the parent updates.
bestPractices: Memoize only when profiler proves a need
Pair with React.memo on the consumer
questions.interviewTip: Say 'referential equality' — that's the whole reason these exist.

questions.title: Controlled vs uncontrolled components.
questions.shortAnswer: Controlled: form state lives in React (value + onChange). Uncontrolled: state lives in the DOM, read via refs.
questions.detailedExplanation: Controlled gives you validation, conditional UI, and a single source of truth. Uncontrolled is simpler and often faster for plain forms — React Hook Form leans on it.
questions.realWorldExample: A search-as-you-type input is controlled. A 30-field settings form might use React Hook Form's uncontrolled mode for performance.
commonMistakes: Mixing controlled and uncontrolled on the same input
Re-rendering the whole form on every keystroke
questions.interviewTip: Bring up React Hook Form — current-best-practice signal.

questions.title: How does React's reconciliation algorithm work?
questions.shortAnswer: React diffs trees by element type and key. Same type → update props; different type → unmount and remount. Keys identify children across renders.
questions.detailedExplanation: Fiber breaks reconciliation into interruptible units of work, enabling concurrent features like transitions. Stable keys keep component state across reorders.
questions.realWorldExample: Reordering a draggable list with stable keys preserves child state (input focus, animations). Index keys would lose it.
bestPractices: Keep component types stable to avoid remounts
questions.interviewTip: Mention Fiber and time-slicing — concurrent React is the modern story.
